answer:
**The title "Saqaliya" of Allama Iqbal's poem (from *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, chapter 21) directly names and addresses the island of Sicily itself, personified throughout as a grieving entity bearing the ruins of "Hejazi civilization" (the Muslim Arab cultural legacy there, from the era of Islamic rule).**[^1][^2][^3]

The poem opens by urging tears for this lost civilization—"the grave of Hejazi civilization"—referencing the Arab Muslims ("desert-dwellers") whose conquests revived a "dead world," shook emperors, and freed humanity from "superstition," with echoes of their *takbir* (Islamic proclamation of God).[^4][^5] Iqbal then apostrophizes Sicily mid-poem: "Ah, O Sicily! You are the honor of the sea," praising its beauty while lamenting its past as "the cradle of that nation's culture" (the Muslim *qaum*, or community).[^6][^7][^8] He positions himself as "pain incarnate," "the dust of that caravan" (Muslim conquerors), tasked by fate to inherit its mourning and "carry [it] as a gift to Hindustan," weeping there himself to stir others.[^9][^10]

**Using the Arabic name "Saqaliya" (صقلية, classical in Islamic texts and history) addresses Sicily through its Islamic-Arabic identity, resonating with Iqbal's Urdu-speaking Muslim audience familiar with that heritage (e.g., via references to Granada, Baghdad, Shiraz's nightingale, and Ibn Badrun).**[^11] A title like "Sicily" (the Latin/Italian *Sicilia*, rendered phonetically as اسسلی or "Sisili" even in the Urdu poem)[^12] **would have failed to immediately evoke this specific Muslim historical and emotional connection—treating the island as a neutral Western geography rather than a symbol of lost Islamic glory.** The corpus contains no explicit authorial commentary on the title choice, but the poem's content makes this cultural intent clear.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 "Saqaliya" (transliteration variant), [title and frontmatter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/transliteration/#p-d17409).
[^2]: *Ibid.*, (translation variant), [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/).
[^3]: *Ibid.*, (original Urdu variant), [title: صقليہ](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/original/#p-dae1ec).
[^4]: *Ibid.*, translation, [p-08760e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e).
[^5]: *Ibid.*, [p-13e58a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-13e58a) to [p-26728b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-26728b).
[^6]: *Ibid.*, [p-379aeb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-379aeb).
[^7]: *Ibid.*, [p-82cbe3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-82cbe3).
[^8]: *Ibid.*, transliteration, [corresponding lines](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/transliteration/#p-d17409).
[^9]: *Ibid.*, translation, [p-a060f8](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-a060f8) to [p-61a01e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-61a01e).
[^10]: *Ibid.*, [p-665fb2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-665fb2).
[^11]: *Ibid.*, original, [p-2ac079](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/original/#p-2ac079) ff.; cf. historical laments for Granada [p-02c3ef](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-02c3ef).
[^12]: *Ibid.*, transliteration: "Aah ae Sicily!" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/transliteration/#p-d17409); original: اے سسلي (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/original/#p-c5c3c0).
